title: Section 73: the power and its two hard limits
paras: Let's begin with section 73 — the power itself, and its two hard limits. Section 73 permits an application, in the words of the statute, "for planning permission for the development of land without complying with conditions subject to which a previous planning permission was granted". On such an application, the authority considers only the conditions subject to which permission should be granted — that's section 73, subsection 2. It may grant subject to different conditions, subject to none at all, or refuse, if it decides the original conditions should stand. It's not an occasion to revisit the principle of development. But the authority must have regard to the development plan and material considerations as they stand at the date of the section 73 decision — and those may have moved since the original grant.

Two statutory limits are built into the section. The first: it's confined to conditions. It presupposes an existing permission carrying a condition to vary or remove, and it can't be used where there's no relevant condition to alter. The second — and this one is frequently overlooked — is section 73, subsection 5, which prohibits a grant that has effect to change a condition by extending the time within which development must be started, or reserved matters applied for. So section 73 cannot extend the life of a permission. The old practice of "renewing" a permission by varying its commencement condition ended when section 73, subsection 5 took effect.

Now, a section 73 grant does not amend the original permission. It creates a new, free-standing permission that exists alongside the original — and the original remains extant unless and until it lapses. The developer may choose which to implement. That duality matters in two directions. Where the original has been partly built, the section 73 permission — which is a permission for the whole development as varied — must be capable of coherent implementation over the whole site. And where two permissions subsist over the same land, the Pilkington principle, which we'll come to in topics five and six, governs whether implementing one forecloses the other.

One point of controversy is timing. A section 73 application may certainly be made while the original permission is extant. Whether one may be made after the original's time limit for implementation has expired is far less certain. The orthodox and prudent view is that section 73 operates on a subsisting permission, and the application should be made before the original lapses. Don't assume an expired permission can be revived by section 73.

title: The description limit: Finney and Armstrong
paras: Next, the description limit — and the two cases that draw it, Finney and Armstrong. The single most important constraint on section 73 is that it cannot alter the description of the development in the operative part of the permission. That is the ratio of Finney against the Welsh Ministers, reported at 2019, E-W-C-A Civ 1868. The permission described two wind turbines with a tip height "up to 100 metres". A section 73 application sought to raise the permitted height to 125 metres by varying the relevant condition. The Court of Appeal — Lord Justice Lewison — held the grant unlawful. Section 73, subsection 2 confines the exercise to the conditions, and gives no power to change the description of the development for which permission was originally granted. If the description must change, section 73 is the wrong tool. The applicant needs a fresh application — or section 73B, once it's in force.

Section 73 is often labelled the route for a "minor material amendment", but that label describes common practice, not a legal limit. Armstrong against the Secretary of State, reported at 2023, E-W-H-C 176, Admin, confirmed that section 73 contains no statutory ceiling on the extent of change. The claimant held permission for a single dwelling, and applied to vary the approved-plans condition to alter the building's architectural design. The authority, and then an inspector, both refused — on the footing that the changes were too material for section 73. The High Court held they had misdirected themselves. The materiality of the changes was irrelevant to the availability of the power, provided the description of development — "a single dwelling" — was unchanged, and the varied conditions stayed consistent with it.

Read together, the two decisions fix one axis of the modern boundary. The size of the change is not what matters — consistency with the operative description is. Even fundamental changes to an approved scheme may be made by section 73, so long as the words describing the development in the operative part aren't contradicted. And this is why the drafting of the original description, years earlier, quietly determines how much flexibility a scheme will ever have.

The practical corollary runs upstream, to every new application. A tightly drawn description — "erection of 14 dwellings in accordance with the approved plans" — is a straitjacket. A loose one — "residential development" — leaves room to vary conditions substantially later, without a fresh application. The description is not boilerplate. It is the width of the door through which every future amendment must pass.

title: Fiske and Lambeth: conditions cannot negate or omit the grant
paras: Now to Fiske and Lambeth — and the rule that conditions cannot negate the grant, or quietly fall out of it. If Finney bars changing the description directly, Test Valley Borough Council against Fiske, reported at 2024, E-W-C-A Civ 1541, closes the obvious workaround: you cannot achieve by condition what you cannot achieve to the description. The original permission was for a solar farm with a substation. The operative part referred to a substation, the approved plans showed a 33 kilovolt substation, and condition 15 required its details. A section 73 permission kept the word "substation" in the description, but removed it from the approved plans and deleted condition 15. The challenge succeeded. Lord Justice Holgate held that a planning permission comprises the operative part and its conditions, and it is unlawful for a condition to "derogate from or negate" the operative grant. The operative part cannot give consent with one hand, only for a condition to take it away with the other. A section 73 permission that's internally inconsistent with its own operative part is bad.

Fiske also confirmed the corollary that is decisive for phased schemes: a condition on one permission cannot lawfully modify a separate permission. The two grants are legally distinct, and one cannot be used to reach across and amend the other. That principle returns in topic six, on drop-in permissions.

How a section 73 permission is read — particularly where the notice omits a condition — was settled by R, on the application of Lambeth, against the Secretary of State, reported at 2019, U-K-S-C 33. Lambeth granted a section 73 permission varying a condition on a retail unit but, through a drafting error, failed to re-impose the condition restricting use to non-food goods. The Supreme Court held the use had not thereby become unrestricted. Construing the permission as a whole, through the reasonable reader with knowledge of the planning history, "the limited description of the use must be read as if it were itself a condition" — the words there are Lord Carnwath's. The restriction survived the omission.

The point is easy to state backwards, so let me state it carefully. Lambeth is not authority that an omitted condition falls away — the Supreme Court reversed the Court of Appeal on exactly that. It is authority that a section 73 permission is construed in full context, so a restriction may keep binding even if it isn't restated. The safe practitioner course is unchanged. Re-state, in terms, every condition intended to continue, and rely on neither the presence nor the absence of a condition to change the substance. A consolidated decision notice — the full description, the entire set of conditions as varied — is the discipline that makes the permission readable, and implementable, as one document.

title: Section 73A: permission for what is already built
paras: Section 73A next — permission for what is already built. Section 73A is a distinct power, constantly confused with section 73 because of the numbering, but it does something quite different: it authorises planning permission for development already carried out. Under section 73A, subsection 2, permission may be granted in three situations. First, for development carried out without permission. Second, for development carried out without complying with a condition. And third, for development carried out before the expiry of a time-limited permission, where retention beyond that period is sought. A section 73A permission may be expressed to take effect from a date earlier than the grant — including the date the development was carried out — and that is what makes it genuinely retrospective, rather than a fresh prospective consent. It's the statutory basis of the "retrospective planning application".

Applications arise in two typical situations. There's the landowner volunteering to regularise development already built — a barn conversion completed otherwise than in accordance with approved plans, say, or an unauthorised change of use. And there's the landowner responding to the threat, or the service, of enforcement. The two routes to authorising past development overlap: the same result can often be reached through the ground A deemed application on an appeal against an enforcement notice under section 174, which invites the Secretary of State to grant permission for the development as carried out. The tactical choice between a proactive section 73A application and reliance on a ground A appeal turns on cost, timing, and the client's risk appetite.

Two points of professional caution. Making a section 73A application is not, in law, an admission that permission was required — but it is often treated as a practical acceptance that the development is unauthorised, and it should not be made reflexively. And section 73A does not cure a permission that was never lawfully implemented. It grants a new permission for what has been done, the merits are assessed afresh, and an authority is entitled to refuse — leaving the development liable to enforcement.

The merits-afresh point is the one clients find hardest. Retrospective does not mean forgiven. The authority weighs the development as if it were newly proposed, and the fact that it's already built earns no planning credit. A scheme that would have been refused prospectively will be refused retrospectively — and the money spent building it is no part of the balance.

title: Phased schemes: the whole-scheme problem
paras: Now, phased schemes — and the whole-scheme problem. Large developments are rarely built exactly as first permitted. Markets move, a phase is re-planned, a house type is substituted, an access shifts. The instinctive tool is section 73: vary a condition and carry on. For a single-permission phased or masterplan scheme, that instinct is now dangerous — because two independent lines of authority converge to constrain it. The first is the Finney and Fiske limit on section 73 itself. The second, more fundamental, is the Pilkington and Hillside rule that a permission for a multi-unit development is normally a single, indivisible whole. So a change to one part is, in law, a change to the entire permission.

The foundation is Pilkington against the Secretary of State, reported at 1973, 1 W-L-R 1527. Where two permissions subsist over the same land, and it is physically impossible to carry out the development authorised by one because of what has been done under the other, the first cannot lawfully be implemented or continued. The test is physical possibility on the ground, judged against what the earlier permission, read as a whole, actually authorised. Pilkington is why a developer cannot hold a portfolio of overlapping permissions and build a hybrid of all of them. Implementing one may extinguish the practical ability to build the others.

The second building block is Sage against the Secretary of State, reported at 2003, U-K-H-L 22, which established the holistic approach to construing a permission. A permission for a building or operation authorises the development as a whole — not as a series of independently lawful fragments. A developer cannot pick and choose which parts of a permitted scheme to implement, and treat the built parts as separately authorised. Sage supplies the conceptual foundation — decisive in Hillside — that a masterplan permission is a single integrated grant, not a collection of standalone consents for each plot or phase.

Put the two propositions together, and you can see the trap. Because the permission is one whole — that's Sage — and because implementing an inconsistent later permission can make the original physically impossible to complete — that's Pilkington — the ordinary developer instinct, get a separate consent for the troublesome phase and carry on with the rest, is exactly the move that can sterilise the original permission. The next topic is the modern authority that drew these threads together.

title: Hillside, Dennis and drop-in permissions
paras: So to Hillside, Dennis, and drop-in permissions. The threads were drawn together by the Supreme Court in Hillside Parks Limited against Snowdonia National Park Authority, reported at 2022, U-K-S-C 30 — the decisive modern authority on amending large schemes. A 1967 permission authorised 401 dwellings on a 28-acre site, to a detailed master plan. Over the decades, a series of further permissions authorised departures from that master plan — until the original layout could no longer be built as approved. The Supreme Court held the 1967 permission could no longer be relied on, and it laid down propositions of general application. First, a multi-unit permission is normally construed as an integrated whole, not a bundle of severable permissions. Second, where later development makes it physically impossible to complete the original as approved, the original cannot lawfully be continued — Pilkington affirmed. Third, the departure must be material — a truly minor variation will not sterilise the original. And fourth, severability is possible, but it must be express, on the terms of the particular permission — never presumed. F Lucas and Sons against Dorking U-D-C, reported in 1966 at 17 P-and-C-R 111, was not disturbed.

Hillside also addressed "drop-in" permissions — a separate permission for part of a larger site, substituting for a portion of the original scheme. It imposed no blanket prohibition. But it made clear that a drop-in does not amend the original permission, and that implementing it may, through Pilkington, defeat the ability to complete the rest. The suggested orthodox route to amend a large scheme is instead a section 73 application varying the whole permission — re-granting the entire development as amended — subject, always, to the Finney bar on changing the description.

The post-Hillside practical fallout was tested in R, on the application of Dennis, against the London Borough of Southwark, reported at 2024, E-W-H-C 57, Admin. The Aylesbury Estate regeneration was consented by a phased outline permission. Facing the Hillside problem, the developer sought a section 96A non-material amendment to insert the word "severable" — so that a later drop-in for one phase would not imperil the rest. Mr Justice Holgate held it would not work. Phasing alone does not connote severability, and — in his words — "simply inserting the bare term 'severable' into a permission does not make it severable". He noted that if phasing equalled severability, the statutory implementation time limits would apply separately to each phase — so some phases could become time-expired, a result no one intended. And converting the permission into a severable one was a material change, beyond section 96A.

The lesson of Dennis is stark. Severability must be engineered into a permission at the outset, by deliberate drafting — it cannot readily be retrofitted onto an existing phased consent. Drop-ins remain possible, but hazardous, and the risks are three. First, a drop-in does not vary the original, which still requires completion as a whole. Second, once the drop-in is implemented, Pilkington and Hillside may render the balance of the original physically incapable of lawful completion. And third, Fiske confirms that a condition on the original cannot be used to authorise or accommodate the separate drop-in. Where a drop-in is pursued, it should cover enough of the site to leave a coherent, implementable whole — and the planning assessment should address the combined development scenario, not the parcel in isolation.

title: Choosing the route — and the coming s73B
paras: Finally, choosing the route — and the coming section 73B. The route follows from one pivotal question, asked first: does the change touch the description of development? If it does, section 73 is unavailable — that's Finney — and the choice is a fresh application or, once in force, section 73B. If it does not, section 73 is available for changes of any magnitude — that's Armstrong — provided the result is internally consistent — that's Fiske. Genuinely non-material changes go to section 96A — but not a change that inserts severability, or is otherwise material — that's Dennis. Extending time for commencement or reserved matters is barred under section 73, subsection 5, and needs a fresh application. And regularising development already built is section 73A — merits assessed afresh, overlapping with section 174 ground A.

For a phased or masterplan scheme specifically, a practitioner needing to change it has, in mid-2026, four realistic options. First, vary the whole permission by section 73 — the orthodox Hillside route — re-granting the whole scheme as amended. That works only where the description is unchanged, per Finney, and the result is consistent, per Fiske. Second, a fresh full permission for the whole site — where the description must change, or the scheme has moved substantially — which avoids the Pilkington and Hillside trap by re-permitting the whole coherently. Third, engineer severability at the outset for new masterplans — after Dennis, the only reliable way to preserve flexibility. Or fourth, a carefully structured drop-in — viable in limited cases, with the safeguards above, and a clear-eyed assessment of the Pilkington risk to the remainder.

Section 111 of the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 inserts a new section 73B into the 1990 Act — widely understood as the intended legislative answer to Hillside and Finney. It will permit an application to vary an existing permission, including its description of development, provided the authority is satisfied that the development as varied is "not substantially different" from that already permitted. It's designed to occupy the ground between section 96A — the non-material route — and a fresh full application, giving large phased schemes a lawful means of material-but-modest amendment that section 73 cannot deliver, because of Finney.

As at mid-2026, section 73B is not yet in force. It awaits commencement regulations, and the supporting procedural, fee and consultation framework. Until it is commenced, the four options above remain the working toolkit, and the Hillside analysis must be applied to every material change to a phased scheme. The uncertain frontier — and I flag it honestly — is the precise test for when a phased permission is severable. Hillside and Dennis establish that it is possible, and that it must be express — but not the drafting that reliably achieves it.
